# Transcrição: 2. Always Present Your Proposal in Writing

- **Fonte:** 2. Always Present Your Proposal in Writing
- **Duração:** 02:56
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 02:56]

Okay, the second rule of closing. Always present your proposal in writing. People believe what they see, not what they hear. People do not believe just the words that are spoken. They believe what they can actually see. It gets validated. That which is written is more credible and more valued than that which is simply just talk or spoken. Remember the old saying, talk is cheap. Hey, it plays out big here in the close. So this is what I want. Always always have a contract available, a legal pad next to you on which you can make your points. You want the contract, the pen and a legal pad. The legal pad makes your points. It supports anything you're saying. Anything offered, any points of value that are included or might be expected should be written down for everyone to see. Example, I'll give you an example. If your product or service has a warranty that is standard in your offering, still write it down. By the way, it has a warranty. It's the easiest five years, whatever the deal is. You want to write down, validate, verify in writing what people are getting because it becomes more real to them and they value it more. This is overlooked probably 99% of all negotiators in all transactions miss this one simple point. Write down what you're saying. The salesperson in the closer assumes, since it's included and everybody includes it, it shouldn't be disclosed. Now I'm talking about two issues here. One, you didn't disclose it. Number two, you didn't write it down. Second reason for not closing is you didn't write it down. I'm just giving you a little tip here. Disclose it. I don't care if you're selling a product, everybody offers the same thing on this product, okay? Free warranty for a year. I'm still going to write it down. The other 99 people they shop won't even write it down. They assume the customer knows when the customer doesn't. Anything of value should be accentuated, written down and brought to attention. You want to shine the light on the positive. It should be used to build your case of logic and you can only get logical with people if they see it in writing because otherwise they're not going to trust it, right? You're giving people reasons to want to do business with you. The more reasons you can give them, the easier they're going to be able to make sense of saying let's do this. Any offers, write them down. Anything included, write it down. Things that you think are common to your proposal, write it down. How they're going to title the vehicle, write it down. How they're going to title the product, write it down. Whose name it might be in. Any other names, write it down. Anything you use to substantiate your offer, write it down. Any comparisons you might make, write it down. Also, lastly, while you're writing, you might want to consider sitting next to your buyer rather than across from your buyer but we'll talk about that in a later segment, okay? Remember, people believe what they see, not what they hear. If you say it, write it.
